title: Why setbacks in recovery are not failures but recalibrations
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The day I stopped calling it failure

I remember the morning exactly. I had been doing so well. Seven months of consistent therapy, morning pages, yoga, boundary-setting. My nervous system had started to feel almost... safe. Then my mother called. Three minutes into the conversation, I felt the familiar collapse in my chest. By the time I hung up, I was curled on the bathroom floor, shaking, flooded with shame so thick I could taste it.

And my first thought was: I failed. I failed at recovery. I failed at healing. I failed at being a person who could handle a simple phone call without disintegrating.

Here's the thing. That thought was a lie. A well-rehearsed, trauma-informed, deeply familiar lie. But a lie nonetheless.

What actually happened on that bathroom floor was not failure. It was a recalibration. My nervous system, which Stephen Porges has spent decades teaching us about through his Polyvagal Theory, was doing exactly what it evolved to do. It detected a cue of danger and activated a survival response. That's not failure. That's biology. That's a system working exactly as designed.

The problem wasn't the collapse. The problem was my interpretation of the collapse. I had bought into the wellness industry's toxic promise that healing is a straight line upward, that setbacks are signs we're doing it wrong, that real recovery looks like constant progress. That's garbage. Pure, marketable, sell-you-another-course garbage.

Think about it. When you learn to walk, you fall. Hundreds of times. Nobody calls those falls failures. They're part of the process. But somehow we expect our nervous systems to learn new patterns without ever stumbling. Wild, right?

What your nervous system is actually doing during a "setback"

Let me get specific about what's happening inside your body when you experience what you're calling a setback. Because understanding the mechanics changes everything.

Your nervous system has one job: keep you alive. That's it. It doesn't care about your goals, your healing timeline, your therapist's expectations, or your Instagram feed. It cares about survival. And it makes decisions about safety faster than your conscious mind can process.

Peter Levine, the brilliant mind behind Somatic Experiencing, calls this the "felt sense." Your body is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. When it detects something familiar from your past trauma - a tone of voice, a facial expression, a specific feeling of powerlessness - it activates a response before you can even think about it.

So when you find yourself dissociating during a conversation. Or snapping at someone you love. Or numbing out with food or screens or substances. Or collapsing into that familiar hopelessness. Your nervous system isn't failing. It's succeeding at its primary mission. It's protecting you from what it perceives as a threat.

Here's the uncomfortable truth. Your trauma responses kept you alive. They got you through. They were brilliant adaptations. And now, when they activate in situations that are actually safe, it feels like betrayal. But it's not betrayal. It's a system that hasn't yet learned that the danger is past.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She writes about how we can meet our own pain with compassion instead of more criticism. That bathroom floor moment? I could have met it with kindness instead of self-flagellation. Radical acceptance doesn't mean giving up. It means seeing clearly what's happening without adding a story of failure on top of it.

Stay with me here. Because the real damage isn't the setback itself. The real damage is what happens after. The shame spiral. The "I'm broken" story. The decision to quit therapy or stop doing your practices because "what's the point." That secondary wounding is often worse than the original activation.

Recalibration vs. failure: the practical difference

So how do you actually tell the difference between a genuine failure and a recalibration? Here's what I've learned from my own recovery and from studying the work of Janina Fisher, who integrates trauma theory with parts work.

Failure is when you give up. When you decide the goal is impossible and stop trying. When you let the setback define your entire trajectory. Failure is a story you tell yourself about permanence. "I'll never get better." "This always happens." "I'm broken."

Recalibration is when you notice the setback, feel the feelings, and then adjust your approach. It's recognizing that your nervous system is giving you information. Maybe you pushed too hard. Maybe you need more support. Maybe this particular trigger needs more time and gentleness. Maybe you're asking your system to do something it's not ready for.

I see this all the time in my own life and in the lives of people who write to me. Someone has a good week. They feel hopeful. They think "I'm healed!" and then they push harder. They take on more. They stop doing their grounding practices because they feel so good. And then something small happens and they crash. And they think "I failed."

No. You overestimated your capacity. That's not failure. That's data. Your nervous system just told you, very clearly, "Hey, I'm not there yet. I need more slow, consistent practice before I can handle that level of stress."

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers specific exercises for mapping your nervous system's responses and finding ways to return to regulation. It's not about never getting dysregulated. It's about knowing how to find your way back.

Think about it like this. When you're learning a musical instrument, you don't expect to play perfectly every time. You expect wrong notes. You expect your fingers to fumble. You expect to need to slow down, repeat sections, go back to basics. And when you hit a wrong note, you don't throw the instrument across the room and declare yourself a failure. You pause. You adjust. You try again.

Recovery from complex trauma is infinitely more complicated than learning piano. So why do we expect perfection?

The shame that keeps you stuck

Here's what I want you to really hear. The most dangerous part of a setback is not the activation itself. It's the shame that follows. The inner voice that says "you should be further along by now." The comparison to other people's recovery timelines. The belief that your setbacks prove something fundamental about your worth.

This is where the wellness industry has done tremendous damage. All those Instagram posts about "rising strong" and "glowing up" and "becoming your best self." They create a fantasy of recovery that has no room for the messy, nonlinear, humiliating reality of actually healing from developmental trauma.

Gabor Mate has said that trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. And I would add that the secondary trauma of shaming yourself for your trauma responses is often more debilitating than the original wounds.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Dr. Arielle Schwartz. It includes exercises for tracking your triggers and developing self-compassion responses. The key is that it doesn't pathologize the setbacks. It normalizes them as part of the process.

I want to offer you a different framework. Instead of asking "Why am I failing?", try asking "What is this setback trying to teach me about my nervous system's current capacity?" Instead of "I should be better by now," try "My system is telling me it needs something different right now." Instead of "I'm broken," try "I'm having a trauma response, and that's information, not indictment."

Notice what just happened in your body when you read those alternatives. Did something soften? Did your shoulders drop even slightly? That's your nervous system responding to a cue of safety. The language we use with ourselves matters at a physiological level.

Building a recovery that has room for setbacks

So what does it actually look like to build a recovery practice that anticipates recalibrations instead of punishing them?

First, you stop expecting linear progress. You accept that healing is spiral-shaped. You'll circle back to familiar territory, but each time you'll be at a slightly different elevation. You'll recognize the old patterns, but you'll have more tools, more awareness, more capacity to meet them differently.

Second, you build in buffer. You don't schedule intense therapy sessions right before important meetings. You don't push your window of tolerance to its absolute limit and then expect to function normally. You leave room for your nervous system to wobble.

Third, you develop a relationship with your parts. Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model suggests that the part of you that collapses in shame is not your enemy. It's a protector. It's trying to keep you safe in the only way it knows. When you befriend that part instead of fighting it, the whole system starts to relax.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay C. Gibson. Understanding why your nervous system developed these patterns in the first place can reduce the shame around them significantly.

Fourth, you practice returning. Not staying regulated all the time. Not achieving permanent calm. Just returning. Over and over. That's the skill. That's the practice. Can you notice when you've left your window of tolerance? Can you find one small way to come back? Can you do that without adding a story of failure?

I'm not going to tell you that setbacks stop hurting. They don't. They still feel like death when they happen. The shame still rises. The old voices still scream that you're hopeless. But what changes is your relationship to those experiences. You stop adding injury to injury. You stop making it worse by attacking yourself for being human.

And slowly, over time, the setbacks become shorter. Less intense. You recover faster. You recognize them earlier. You have more compassion for yourself in the middle of them. That's not failure. That's the work. That's the actual, messy, unglamorous, deeply real work of healing a nervous system that was wounded in relationship and can only heal in relationship - including the relationship you have with yourself.

I still have bathroom floor moments. They're less frequent now. Less catastrophic. I've learned to lie there and say "Oh, there you are. I know you. You're trying to protect me. Thank you. But I'm actually safe now. We can come back when you're ready."

That's not failure. That's recalibration. And it's the most honest, most sustainable recovery I know.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - Dr. Arielle Schwartz's structured workbook for C-PTSD survivors
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Lindsay C. Gibson's validating book on childhood experiences
